ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pork rib food product class; no specific braised-with-sauce variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific braised pork ribs with sauce entry; A00LC (Pig meat products) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for braised pork ribs with sauce identified in the clinical terminology.

main_ingredients: pork ribs (大排 / pork loin chop on bone)

serving.description: 1 serving (1–2 pork rib pieces with sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of braised pork ribs with sauce consists of one to two bone-in pork rib pieces with sauce, weighing approximately 250 g.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, loin, blade (chops), bone-in, cooked, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10062); adapted with sauce contribution estimated from USDA condiment entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy entry for bone-in braised pork loin/rib (NDB 10062: pork loin blade chop braised). Sauce contribution (soy sauce, sugar, oyster sauce) estimated from USDA FoodData Central condiment entries and blended proportionally at approximately 15% sauce weight fraction. Sodium elevated to reflect soy-sauce-based braising liquid. Values represent a composite estimate for the finished dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-and-sauce dishes — analogous estimate based on braised pork / meat-based dishes with soy sauce; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised pork ribs with sauce. GI estimated at 35 based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein, high-fat profile of the dish. Analogous reference: braised/stewed meat dishes with soy-based sauces have GI in the 30–40 range per University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar mixed meat dishes. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 4.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce braising liquid (~620 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1550 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100–150 g and request reduced-sauce servings, as the soy-sauce-based braising liquid contributes substantially to sodium intake, potentially exceeding 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit in a single restaurant serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content from sauce (sugar, cornstarch) with low glycemic index (GI ~35); high fat content may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (~35) and moderate carbohydrate load (~4.8 g per 100 g) make this dish relatively manageable for blood glucose control, but the high saturated fat content (~4.8 g per 100 g) and added sugars in the sauce warrant portion control and pairing with non-starchy vegetables to support overall glycaemic management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~4.8 g/100 g), cholesterol (~72 mg/100 g), and high sodium (~620 mg/100 g) from braising sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently and in small portions, as the combination of saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and high sodium from the soy-based sauce presents a compounded cardiovascular risk; choosing leaner cuts and requesting sauce on the side can help reduce overall lipid and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Pork Ribs with Sauce (烧汁大排, Shāo zhī dà pái) is a classic Chinese braised pork dish popular in Chinese restaurants across Singapore and mainland China. The dish features bone-in pork rib sections — typically the large loin chop cut known as 大排 (dà pái) — that are first marinated and then slow-braised in a rich, glossy sauce made from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, sugar, garlic, and ginger. The braising process renders the pork tender and deeply flavoured, with the sauce reducing to a thick, caramelised coating. The texture is succulent and slightly sticky, with a savoury-sweet umami profile characteristic of Chinese red-braised pork preparations. Nutritionally, the dish is a significant source of protein (~19.5 g/100 g), moderate-to-high in fat (~13.2 g/100 g, including ~4.8 g saturated fat), and contains a notable amount of sodium (~620 mg/100 g) due to the soy-sauce-based braising liquid. The carbohydrate content is low (~4.8 g/100 g), primarily from added sugar and cornstarch in the sauce, giving the dish a low glycemic index (~35). Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the saturated fat and cholesterol content are relevant for cardiovascular risk management. The dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets. It is commonly served as a main course with steamed rice in Chinese zi char restaurants and family-style dining establishments in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for braised pork ribs with sauce found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy braised bone-in pork loin/rib entry (NDB 10062) with sauce contribution estimated from USDA condiment data. GI is an analogous estimate based on similar braised meat dishes. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Meat and alternatives', as braised pork ribs is a meat dish, not a condiment. 2. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; an exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry was not identified for this specific dish. USDA SR Legacy NDB 10062 (pork loin blade chop, braised) used as the primary base with sauce adjustment. 3. Sodium value (~620 mg/100 g) reflects the soy-sauce-heavy braising liquid and is a key clinical concern. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein, high-fat profile and analogous braised meat dish entries. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (5.6), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (310.0), calcium_mg (18.0), iron_mg (1.2), cholesterol_mg (72.0), vitamin_c_mg (0.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (4.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.45), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.22), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (4.8), vitamin_b6_mg (0.38), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.65), folate_mcg_dfe (5.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.4), vitamin_e_mg (0.3), phosphorus_mg (185.0), magnesium_mg (22.0), zinc_mg (2.8), selenium_mcg (28.0), water_g (58.5) are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 10062 adapted values; no -1 sentinel values are used as the USDA source provides reasonable estimates for all reported micronutrients. 6. No core macros are zero; all four core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero and consistent with a braised pork rib dish.
